journal_entry.rationale: When a customer deposit (deferred liability) was originally received, it was recorded as a credit to Deferred Revenue / Customer Deposits. No cash changes hands at this point. No tax line is added here because HST/GST would have been collected and recorded when the deposit was originally received.

In a corporate context, customer deposits are initially recorded as deferred (unearned) revenue — a liability. Upon delivery of the service, that liability is extinguished and the revenue is recognized. The ambiguity was whether this was a new payment or a revenue recognition event; business context (corporation, service delivery language) confirms it is the latter.

No tax applies to share repurchases in Canada. Total debits = total credits = $200.

output_approver.reason: Entry correctly records a treasury stock repurchase. (1) Balance verified: total debits ($200 Treasury Stock) = total credits ($200 Cash). (2) Accounts are appropriate: Treasury Stock is the correct contra-equity account for a share buyback, and Cash is the correct funding account. (3) Amount matches the transaction text ($200). (4) Directionality is correct: Treasury Stock is debited (contra-equity increases, net equity decreases) and Cash is credited (asset decreases). (5) No tax lines are required — share repurchases are not subject to GST/HST in Canada. No issues found.

journal_entry.description: Inventory destroyed by typhoon — $2,000 loss recognized
journal_entry.rationale: The typhoon destroyed inventory, resulting in a $2,000 loss. Per the debit tuple (0,0,1,0,0,0), one expense line is required — this is recorded as a Casualty Loss (or similar extraordinary/other loss expense account), as the destruction of inventory is an involuntary, non-operating loss event. Per the credit tuple (0,0,0,1,0,0), one asset decrease line is required — Inventory is credited to remove the destroyed goods from the books at their carrying value. No tax applies as this is an internal write-off, not a purchase or sale transaction.

output_disambiguator.reason: Transaction describes an involuntary inventory loss due to a natural disaster. Corporate business context confirms this is a business asset write-off, not a sale or transfer. Clarified as a casualty/disaster loss write-off for downstream classification.

output_debit_classifier.reason: The typhoon destruction of inventory is an involuntary, uninsured loss — this is classified as a casualty/loss expense increase (e.g., "Loss from Natural Disaster" or "Casualty Loss" expense account). The credit side (reduction of Inventory asset) is handled separately. There is only one debit line: the expense increase for the loss recognized.

output_credit_classifier.reason: Inventory (an asset) is physically destroyed and removed from the books — this is an asset decrease on the credit side. The debit side records the loss expense, but the credit here is solely the reduction of the inventory asset.

output_debit_classifier.reason: Accruing a utility bill means recognizing the expense before cash is paid. This creates 1 debit line: Utility Expense (expense increase). The offsetting credit goes to Accrued Liabilities, but that is handled by the credit-side agent.

journal_entry.description: Issue common stock for $180 cash — $100 par value, $80 additional paid-in capital
journal_entry.rationale: The company receives $180 cash (1 asset debit line). The proceeds are split into two equity credit lines per the tuple Credit(0,2,0,0,0,0): Common Stock at par value ($100) and Additional Paid-In Capital for the excess above par ($80). This transaction is a capital contribution — no goods or services are exchanged, so no tax applies.

The breakdown of $100 par value and $80 APIC removes all ambiguity — this is a share issuance, not a revenue deposit or loan. Corporation ownership context confirms this is a standard equity transaction, not an owner draw or sole proprietor contribution.
